title: Quick Performance Wins
intro: You've inherited a slow React project and need to find quick performance wins. Think of it like cleaning your apartment before surprise guests arrive -- a few small fixes can make everything feel dramatically better. Let's use some command-line tools to measure the current state and apply fixes that make an immediate difference.

teach.title: Low-Hanging Fruit First
teach.body: Before you renovate the whole kitchen, check if the fridge door is just left open. Most slow apps have a few obvious problems that account for 80% of the slowness. Fix those first and you'll see dramatic improvement with minimal effort. These are your **quick wins**.

teach.title: Quick Wins = Cleaning Before Guests Arrive
teach.body: When surprise guests are 10 minutes away, you don't renovate the bathroom. You toss clutter in a closet, wipe the counter, and light a candle. It takes 5 minutes but makes the whole place feel 10x better. **Quick performance wins** work the same way -- a few small changes (compress images, defer scripts, add caching) can transform how fast your app feels without rewriting anything.

teach.title: The Top Quick Wins
teach.body:
The fastest ways to speed up most web apps:

1. **Compress images** -- often cuts page weight by 50-80%
2. **Enable gzip/brotli compression** -- shrinks text files (HTML, CSS, JS) by 60-80%
3. **Add caching headers** -- returning visitors don't re-download unchanged files
4. **Remove unused CSS/JS** -- many projects ship code nobody uses
5. **Defer non-critical scripts** -- let the page render before loading analytics and widgets

teach.title: Checking Your Bundle Size
teach.code:
# See what's in your JavaScript bundle
npx webpack-bundle-analyzer dist/stats.json

# Or for a quick size check
du -sh dist/
ls -lh dist/static/js/
teach.annotation: Understanding what's in your bundle is the first step. Often you'll find huge libraries being imported when only a small part is needed.

teach.body: You don't need weeks of refactoring to see results. A single afternoon of quick performance wins can slash load times and immediately boost user retention -- which translates directly to more revenue from your app.

Quick performance wins include compressing images, enabling server compression, adding cache headers, removing unused code, and deferring non-critical scripts. Measure first, then fix the biggest problems.

steps.instruction: First, let's check how big the production build is. Run the build command and see the output sizes.
steps.output:
Creating an optimized production build...

Compiled successfully.

File sizes after gzip:

  582.4 KB  dist/static/js/main.chunk.js
  124.8 KB  dist/static/js/vendors.chunk.js
  8.2 KB    dist/static/css/main.css

Total bundle: 715.4 KB (gzipped)
steps.hint: Run the standard build command for a Node.js project: npm run build

steps.instruction: 582 KB for main.chunk.js is massive! Let's analyze what's inside the bundle to find what's taking up space.
steps.output:
Results:
  moment.js          234.2 KB (40.2%)
  lodash             142.8 KB (24.5%)
  react-icons (all)   98.4 KB (16.9%)
  app code            82.1 KB (14.1%)
  other               24.9 KB  (4.3%)
steps.hint: Use npx source-map-explorer to analyze a JavaScript bundle file and see what's inside.

steps.instruction: Moment.js is 234 KB -- over 40% of the bundle! It's a date library that includes locale data for every language. Let's replace it with a lightweight alternative. Install date-fns instead.
steps.output:
added 1 package in 2.1s

1 package is looking for funding
  run `npm fund` for details
steps.hint: Install the date-fns package which is a modern, lightweight alternative to Moment.js.

steps.instruction: Now let's also check the hero image size. Large unoptimized images are one of the most common performance killers.
steps.hint: Use 'ls -lh' to see the file size in human-readable format.

steps.instruction: 4.2 MB for a single image! Let's convert it to WebP format with compression. This typically cuts image size by 70-80%.
steps.output:
Converted: hero.png -> hero.webp
Original: 4.2 MB
Optimized: 380 KB (91% smaller)
steps.hint: Use an image optimization tool to convert the PNG to WebP format. Try npx sharp-cli.

exercise.completionMessage: Great work! By replacing Moment.js with date-fns and compressing the hero image, you've already cut over 4 MB from the page. The JavaScript bundle shrank by 40%, and the image went from 4.2 MB to 380 KB. These two quick wins alone would noticeably improve load time for every user.
